story:
I’m mid-battle in a video game when my bedroom door flies open. Dad’s in the doorway, Samson—our golden retriever—launches himself into my lap, and my controller goes skidding. By the time I recover, the match is gone. I grumble that I was winning; Dad says I was getting my butt handed to me. I shoot my shot: “Madden? Four-minute quarters?” He almost bites, then scans my desk. “Homework first.”

I drag myself to the disaster that is my notebook, pages jutting out like a paper snowflake. The math assignment isn’t there. My backpack? Empty. I do find last weekend’s homework stuck to a page with something that looks suspiciously like peanut butter, and my stomach drops—I never turned it in. Stakes: raised.

Then a ridiculous, perfect idea hits me. If I can make it look like Samson ate my homework, Dad can’t be mad I don’t have it, and maybe we’ll still play Madden. I hustle to the kitchen. “Can I have some peanut butter?” Dad gives me a look, slices an apple, and plops a spoonful of peanut butter in a bowl. I say I want to eat while doing homework so I can finish fast and destroy him in Madden. He says fine—no mess.

Back in my room, I cue up a show on my phone and start snacking. The apple’s gone before I realize it. So is the peanut butter. I stare at the empty bowl. The plan depends on bait; I’ve eaten the bait. Desperate, I hold my old homework under Samson’s nose. He sniffs the faint peanut butter in the corner, thinks about it for half a second, then turns away like a snob. Okay. Plan B: do the dirty work myself.

I tear the old assignment into jagged pieces, sprinkle a few into my backpack, then clamp the biggest chunk between my teeth and gag my way through slobbering it up and adding bite marks. For realism, I stage the scene and shout, “Samson, no! Bad dog!” Samson bolts down the hall. Dad calls, “What’s going on?” I fake watery eyes and hold up the chewed paper. “He ate my homework!”

Dad’s already in the hallway, smiling. “You never cease to amaze me with how hard you’ll work not to do homework.” My jaw hits the floor. He explains he’s not mad about missing work as much as about the lying. If I’d used this much effort on my homework, I’d be done and we’d be playing Madden.

I confess everything: I thought I left the math at school, I planned to frame Samson with peanut butter, and yes, I slobbered on the paper myself. I’m bracing for a lecture when Samson trots out of the spare room with a sheet in his mouth. I take it—it’s my missing math assignment, the top corner soggy and ragged with tooth marks. Yesterday I must’ve set it down there after asking Dad for help, then forgot. Samson found it before we did.

Dad bursts into a real laugh. “Well, looks like you’ll actually have to turn in a half-eaten math assignment. The dog really did eat your homework.” And I finally get the point: honesty first, effort where it counts, then maybe Madden.
